refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable execution of the load→travel→dump cycle within the customer-defined cycle time target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting/enabling the capability to achieve cycle time, and (2) actually achieving the measured KPI. The refined FR is atomic and solution-neutral by focusing on the functional obligation—enabling the cycle to execute within the time envelope—without prescribing how (e.g., via speed optimization, path planning, operator training, or equipment design). The actual achievement of p95 ≤ CT_target is a verification criterion, not a functional requirement. This separation allows DPs to address enablement independently of operational performance measurement.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that under nominal duty cycle and site conditions, the NGHE solution's design permits completion of load→travel→dump within CT_target. Measure p95 cycle time across representative worksite trials; if p95 ≤ CT_target, the FR is satisfied. If p95 > CT_target, investigate whether the failure is due to (a) inadequate DP (design does not enable the speed/efficiency needed) or (b) operational/environmental factors outside the DP scope.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-002.1: The NGHE solution shall enable load acquisition at the designated load point within the customer-defined load time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.2: The NGHE solution shall enable travel from load point to dump point within the customer-defined travel time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.3: The NGHE solution shall enable payload release at the designated dump point within the customer-defined dump time budget.
FR-NGHE-002.4: The NGHE solution shall minimize idle/transition time between load, travel, and dump phases.

candidate_dps: DP1: Decompose cycle into discrete, sequenced phases (load, travel, dump) with phase-gating logic that enforces strict ordering and allows independent optimization of each phase duration.
DP2: Implement a centralized cycle-time controller that monitors elapsed time against phase budgets and dynamically adjusts operational parameters (speed, acceleration, dwell) to meet p95 target.

DP3: Design modular subsystem interfaces (propulsion, hydraulics, control) such that each subsystem exposes cycle-phase-relevant performance metrics (e.g., load-to-motion latency, travel speed, dump flow rate) enabling closed-loop cycle management.

DP4: Establish a duty-cycle characterization model (load mass distribution, terrain profile, ambient conditions) and pre-compute phase-time budgets offline; validate against measured p95 at commissioning.
DP5: Implement redundant or fail-safe cycle-abort logic that halts progression if any phase exceeds its budget, triggering diagnostic logging and operator alert rather than allowing cycle-time creep.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1 + DP3 + DP4 (integrated approach): Decompose the cycle into discrete, sequenced phases with phase-gating logic (DP1); design modular subsystem interfaces that expose cycle-phase-relevant performance metrics (DP3); establish a duty-cycle characterization model with pre-computed phase budgets validated at commissioning (DP4). This combination decouples phase optimization from global cycle control, enables subsystem independence, and grounds acceptance criteria in measurable, site-specific data.

potential_sources: DP1 ↔ DP3: Phase-gating logic must consume subsystem performance metrics; interface contract must be stable and version-controlled to avoid hidden coupling.
DP3 ↔ DP4: Subsystem metrics (e.g., travel speed) must align with duty-cycle model assumptions; mismatch between modeled and actual performance can invalidate budgets.
DP4 ↔ acceptance criteria: p95 measurement methodology must match the duty-cycle characterization (same load, terrain, conditions); deviation in test conditions invalidates validation.
coupling_check.notes: Coupling is manageable if subsystem interfaces are formally specified (metric definitions, units, update rates) and duty-cycle model is version-controlled with traceability to commissioning data. Risk of latent coupling increases if phase budgets are hardcoded without feedback from actual subsystem performance.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Each phase (load, travel, dump) can be independently optimized without cross-phase interference (e.g., load phase does not constrain travel speed). RISK: If load placement affects vehicle stability during travel, phases are coupled; mitigation: validate independence via simulation or prototype testing.

ASSUMPTION: Subsystem performance (propulsion, hydraulics) is deterministic and repeatable under the same duty-cycle conditions. RISK: Wear, temperature variation, or operator skill variance may cause p95 to drift over time; mitigation: implement periodic re-characterization and adaptive budgets.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite conditions (terrain, load mass distribution) remain within the characterized duty-cycle envelope. RISK: Outlier conditions (e.g., soft ground, overload) will violate p95 target; mitigation: operator training, load-mass verification, and site-specific commissioning.

ASSUMPTION: Phase-gating logic can be implemented with sufficient determinism and latency to not introduce unintended delays. RISK: Control software latency or synchronization issues may add hidden overhead; mitigation: measure and budget control-loop latency explicitly.

ASSUMPTION: Acceptance criteria (p95 under agreed duty cycle) is measurable and repeatable. RISK: Measurement methodology (e.g., sensor accuracy, sample size) may not be agreed upfront; mitigation: define measurement protocol and acceptance test plan before commissioning.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: 'The NGHE solution shall execute the load→travel→dump cycle such that the 95th percentile cycle time, measured under the agreed duty cycle (load mass range, terrain profile, ambient conditions) and site conditions, does not exceed CT_target. Phase-time budgets shall be characterized at commissioning and validated against measured p95 performance. Any deviation exceeding ±5% shall trigger re-characterization or operational constraint adjustment.' This revision grounds the FR in measurable, site-specific acceptance criteria and clarifies the role of DP4 (duty-cycle characterization) in validating the design.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Integrated cycle management system encompassing phase decomposition logic, subsystem interface orchestration, and duty-cycle characterization with phase budgeting and validation

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound: subsystem performance metrics (phase-relevant KPIs, duty-cycle state, phase budget utilization); Outbound: phase-gating decisions, cycle phase transitions, budget validation status

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives mission/cycle requests; NGHE Solution decomposes into phase sequence and validates against pre-computed phase budgets

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits phase-gating holds or cycle abort signals if duty-cycle budgets are exceeded or phase preconditions fail

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Transmits phase state and budget utilization telemetry; receives commissioning-time duty-cycle characterization data and phase budget updates

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: DP1 (phase decomposition + gating) is the core orchestration logic; DP3 (modular subsystem interfaces + metrics) aligns with NGHE's role as solution integrator; DP4 (duty-cycle model + phase budgets) is a commissioning-time characterization artifact naturally owned by the solution layer. Minor diffusion: subsystem implementations (Vehicle, Teleoperation) must conform to phase-relevant metric contracts, but this is interface-level coupling, not logic diffusion.

Poor fit: Vehicle is a physical/functional subsystem, not a control orchestrator. Phase-gating logic and duty-cycle budgeting are cross-subsystem concerns that would fragment if localized to Vehicle. DP3 (subsystem interfaces) requires a neutral arbiter; Vehicle cannot own the interface contract for Teleoperation or Safety Services. High diffusion: phase decomposition logic would leak into Vehicle firmware, and duty-cycle model would require replication across subsystems.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is a human-in-the-loop subsystem, not a cycle orchestrator. Phase-gating and duty-cycle budgeting are independent of teleoperation mode. DP1 (phase decomposition) and DP4 (duty-cycle model) have no natural home in Teleoperation. High diffusion: would require Teleoperation to enforce phase constraints and validate budgets, violating separation of concerns.

Moderate-poor fit: Safety Services owns SafetyAlert interface and can enforce phase-gating holds, but does not own the phase decomposition logic (DP1) or duty-cycle characterization (DP4). DP3 (subsystem interfaces) is not a safety concern per se. Moderate diffusion: phase budgets and gating decisions would need to be computed elsewhere and passed to Safety Services, creating a split responsibility.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The integrated DP (DP1 + DP3 + DP4) is fundamentally a cross-subsystem orchestration and characterization concern. NGHE Solution, as the system-level integration layer, is the natural owner because: (1) phase decomposition and gating (DP1) require a neutral, global view of the cycle; (2) modular subsystem interfaces (DP3) must be defined and validated at the solution level to ensure independence; (3) duty-cycle characterization and phase budgeting (DP4) are commissioning-time artifacts that ground acceptance criteria across all subsystems. Containment is strong because all three DPs address the same problem domain (cycle orchestration and validation) and require unified ownership to avoid logic fragmentation and budget inconsistency.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution may become a monolithic orchestrator if phase-gating logic grows to include subsystem-specific optimizations. Mitigation: enforce strict separation between phase sequencing (NGHE) and subsystem-level phase execution (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services).

Risk: Duty-cycle characterization (DP4) requires site-specific data collection at commissioning; if NGHE Solution lacks a clear interface for ingesting and validating this data, phase budgets may become stale or inconsistent. Mitigation: define a formal commissioning protocol and version-control phase budgets as part of NGHE configuration.

Risk: Subsystem interfaces (DP3) may proliferate if each subsystem defines its own phase-relevant metrics. Mitigation: establish a canonical metric schema at the NGHE level and enforce it via Telemetry interface contracts.

Risk: Phase-gating decisions (DP1) may conflict with real-time subsystem constraints if phase budgets are not validated against actual subsystem capabilities. Mitigation: include subsystem feedback loops in the phase-gating logic and allow dynamic budget adjustment within safety margins.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP choice is necessary. The integrated DP (DP1 + DP3 + DP4) is well-suited to NGHE Solution ownership. However, to strengthen containment and reduce diffusion risk, recommend: (1) explicitly define a 'Phase Orchestration Engine' sub-module within NGHE Solution that owns DP1 logic; (2) establish a 'Subsystem Interface Registry' that owns DP3 metric contracts and validates conformance; (3) create a 'Duty-Cycle Characterization Store' that owns DP4 phase budgets and commissioning validation. These sub-modules should be tightly coupled internally but expose only phase-gating decisions and budget status externally via Telemetry and SafetyAlert interfaces. This preserves the integrated DP while making internal ownership and diffusion boundaries explicit.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criterion 'p95 ≤ CT_target under agreed duty cycle and site conditions' is directly measurable via Telemetry interface (phase-relevant KPIs, phase budget utilization, cycle phase transitions). NGHE Solution owns the phase decomposition and duty-cycle characterization (DP4) that grounds this criterion in site-specific, pre-computed phase budgets validated at commissioning. Verification is feasible because: (1) phase transitions are observable events; (2) duty-cycle state and budget utilization are telemetered; (3) commissioning-time validation establishes baseline acceptance thresholds. Worksite safety plan and operational policies are enforced via SafetyAlert interface (phase-gating holds, cycle abort signals) owned by NGHE Solution in coordination with Safety Services.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. NGHE Solution is the clear owner of the integrated DP (DP1 + DP3 + DP4) and the FR. Allocation is unambiguous: phase decomposition (DP1) and duty-cycle budgeting (DP4) are orchestration concerns that require a neutral, system-level arbiter; modular subsystem interfaces (DP3) must be defined and validated at the solution level to ensure independence and metric consistency. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are subordinate subsystems that conform to phase-relevant metric contracts and respond to phase-gating decisions, but do not own the orchestration logic. This allocation avoids fragmentation and ensures consistent cycle-time validation across all site conditions.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Phase-gating logic (DP1) may leak into Vehicle firmware if subsystem-specific optimizations (e.g., load-phase acceleration profiles, dump-phase timing) are not strictly decoupled from phase sequencing. Mitigation: enforce a clear contract boundary—NGHE Solution owns phase sequencing and gating; Vehicle owns phase execution and performance optimization within phase budgets. Define phase-relevant metrics (e.g., phase duration, energy consumption, position accuracy) as the only coupling mechanism.

Risk: Duty-cycle characterization (DP4) may become inconsistent if subsystem implementations (Vehicle, Teleoperation) maintain local copies of phase budgets or duty-cycle models. Mitigation: establish NGHE Solution as the single source of truth for phase budgets; distribute via Network Link interface at commissioning time and version-control as part of NGHE configuration. Subsystems receive read-only budget allocations, not ownership.

Risk: Subsystem interface contracts (DP3) may proliferate if each subsystem defines its own phase-relevant metrics without coordination. Mitigation: establish a canonical metric schema at the NGHE level (e.g., phase_id, phase_duration_budget, phase_energy_budget, phase_state) and enforce it via Telemetry interface. Use a 'Subsystem Interface Registry' sub-module to validate conformance and prevent metric drift.

Risk: Phase-gating decisions (DP1) may conflict with real-time subsystem constraints if phase budgets are not validated against actual subsystem capabilities during commissioning. Mitigation: include subsystem feedback loops in the phase-gating logic; allow dynamic budget adjustment within safety margins; flag budget violations as SafetyAlert events for operator review.

Risk: NGHE Solution may become a monolithic orchestrator if phase-gating logic grows to include subsystem-specific optimizations or conditional branching. Mitigation: enforce strict separation between phase sequencing (deterministic, pre-computed) and subsystem-level phase execution (adaptive, feedback-driven). Use a 'Phase Orchestration Engine' sub-module to own DP1 logic and expose only phase-gating decisions externally.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.92 score). The integrated DP (DP1 + DP3 + DP4) is fundamentally a cross-subsystem orchestration and characterization concern that naturally belongs to NGHE Solution as the system-level integration layer. All three DPs address the same problem domain (cycle orchestration and validation) and require unified ownership to avoid logic fragmentation and budget inconsistency. Minor diffusion (0.08 score) arises from interface-level coupling: Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services must conform to phase-relevant metric contracts defined by NGHE Solution, but this is expected and manageable via formal interface specifications. To further reduce diffusion risk and strengthen containment, recommend decomposing NGHE Solution into three tightly coupled internal sub-modules: (1) 'Phase Orchestration Engine' owns DP1 (phase decomposition + gating logic); (2) 'Subsystem Interface Registry' owns DP3 (metric contracts + conformance validation); (3) 'Duty-Cycle Characterization Store' owns DP4 (phase budgets + commissioning validation). These sub-modules should be tightly coupled internally but expose only phase-gating decisions and budget status externally via Telemetry and SafetyAlert interfaces. This preserves the integrated DP while making internal ownership and diffusion boundaries explicit.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The integrated DP (DP1 + DP3 + DP4) directly and sufficiently addresses the FR. DP1 (phase decomposition + gating) enables execution of the load→travel→dump cycle by breaking it into discrete, sequenced phases with explicit gating logic that enforces preconditions and prevents out-of-order execution. DP3 (modular subsystem interfaces + metrics) enables subsystem independence and performance visibility, allowing each subsystem to optimize within its phase budget without global coupling. DP4 (duty-cycle characterization + phase budgeting) grounds the cycle-time target in site-specific, pre-computed phase budgets validated at commissioning, ensuring that acceptance criteria (p95 ≤ CT_target) are measurable and achievable. The combination decouples phase optimization from global cycle control, enabling subsystem independence while maintaining cycle-time predictability. No alternative DP is necessary; the integrated approach is sound and well-justified.

recommended_revisions: Decompose NGHE Solution into three explicit internal sub-modules to strengthen containment and clarify ownership: (1) 'Phase Orchestration Engine' (owns DP1: phase decomposition + gating logic); (2) 'Subsystem Interface Registry' (owns DP3: metric contracts + conformance validation); (3) 'Duty-Cycle Characterization Store' (owns DP4: phase budgets + commissioning validation). These sub-modules should be tightly coupled internally but expose only phase-gating decisions and budget status externally.

Define a formal commissioning protocol that specifies how duty-cycle characterization data (DP4) is collected, validated, and ingested into NGHE Solution. Include version control for phase budgets and a rollback mechanism if budgets become stale or inconsistent.

Establish a canonical metric schema for phase-relevant KPIs (e.g., phase_id, phase_duration_budget, phase_energy_budget, phase_state, budget_utilization_pct) and enforce it via Telemetry interface contracts. Use the Subsystem Interface Registry to validate conformance and prevent metric drift.

Enforce strict separation between phase sequencing (deterministic, pre-computed by NGHE Solution) and subsystem-level phase execution (adaptive, feedback-driven by Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services). Document this boundary in interface specifications and code comments.

Include subsystem feedback loops in the phase-gating logic (DP1) to detect real-time constraint violations and allow dynamic budget adjustment within safety margins. Flag budget violations as SafetyAlert events for operator review and commissioning re-validation.

Define explicit phase-gating hold and cycle abort conditions in the SafetyAlert interface. Coordinate with Safety Services to ensure that phase-gating decisions are consistent with worksite safety policies and operational constraints.

decision.justification: Gate results confirm that FR4 and its integrated DP (DP1 + DP3 + DP4) are sound, well-allocated, and feasible to verify. However, the recommended revisions introduce three explicit internal sub-modules (Phase Orchestration Engine, Subsystem Interface Registry, Duty-Cycle Characterization Store) that require decomposition to clarify ownership, reduce diffusion risk, and strengthen containment. Additionally, high safety criticality demands explicit sub-level FRs for phase-gating logic, metric contract enforcement, and commissioning validation. Decomposing now ensures that safety-critical orchestration concerns are properly scoped, verified, and traced to implementation modules before subsystem integration. This is a controlled zigzag: we accept FR4 and DP as correct but decompose to the next level to operationalize the recommended internal structure and safety-critical requirements.
next_level_sub_frs: FR4.1: Decompose and sequence the load→travel→dump cycle into discrete, gated phases with explicit preconditions and state transitions, ensuring no out-of-order execution and deterministic phase ordering.

FR4.2: Enforce phase-gating decisions (hold, proceed, abort) based on real-time subsystem state, phase budget utilization, and safety constraints, with observable gating events and cycle abort signals.

FR4.3: Define and validate modular subsystem interface contracts (phase-relevant metrics, phase budgets, state observability) to ensure subsystem independence and metric consistency across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services.

FR4.4: Characterize site-specific duty cycles and compute phase budgets (duration, energy, position accuracy) at commissioning time, with version control and rollback mechanisms to maintain budget consistency.
FR4.5: Detect real-time phase budget violations and constraint conflicts, trigger SafetyAlert events for operator review, and enable dynamic budget adjustment within safety margins.

FR4.6: Provide observable telemetry of phase state, phase duration utilization, phase energy utilization, and cycle-time progress to enable verification of acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ CT_target) and commissioning validation.

revise.why: Introduce explicit internal sub-module structure within NGHE Solution to operationalize the recommended revisions and clarify ownership boundaries: (1) 'Phase Orchestration Engine' (owns FR4.1 + FR4.2: phase decomposition, gating logic, state transitions); (2) 'Subsystem Interface Registry' (owns FR4.3: metric contracts, conformance validation, interface schema); (3) 'Duty-Cycle Characterization Store' (owns FR4.4: phase budgets, commissioning data, version control); (4) 'Budget Violation Monitor' (owns FR4.5: real-time constraint detection, SafetyAlert coordination); (5) 'Telemetry Aggregator' (owns FR4.6: phase KPI collection, cycle-time tracking, commissioning validation). These sub-modules are tightly coupled internally but expose only phase-gating decisions, budget status, and telemetry externally. This structure reduces diffusion risk, clarifies safety-critical ownership, and enables independent verification of each sub-concern.

assumptions: Phase budgets computed at commissioning time remain valid for the duration of the operational cycle under agreed duty-cycle conditions.
Subsystem feedback (Vehicle phase duration, Teleoperation latency, Safety Services constraints) is available in real-time via Telemetry interface and can be used to detect budget violations.
SafetyAlert interface is reliable and has sufficient latency margin to enforce phase-gating holds and cycle abort signals before safety-critical events occur.
Subsystems (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) conform to phase-relevant metric contracts and do not maintain local copies of phase budgets or duty-cycle models.
Commissioning protocol includes validation of phase budgets against actual subsystem capabilities and site conditions; budgets are not assumed but measured and validated.
Operator review and approval of budget violations is feasible within the operational cycle time; dynamic budget adjustment does not introduce unacceptable delays.
open_risks: Risk: Phase-gating logic may become complex if real-time constraint detection (FR4.5) requires adaptive budget adjustment. Mitigation: define a bounded set of adjustment rules and validate them during commissioning; flag non-standard adjustments as SafetyAlert events for operator review.

Risk: Telemetry latency may prevent timely detection of phase budget violations. Mitigation: establish latency requirements for Telemetry interface during commissioning; include latency margin in phase budget calculations.

Risk: Subsystem implementations may drift from phase-relevant metric contracts if interface specifications are not enforced during development and integration. Mitigation: establish Subsystem Interface Registry as a formal artifact; include interface conformance checks in integration testing and commissioning validation.

Risk: Duty-cycle characterization may become stale if site conditions change (e.g., soil type, load mass, terrain) after commissioning. Mitigation: define a re-commissioning protocol and trigger conditions; include budget staleness detection in the Duty-Cycle Characterization Store.

Risk: Phase Orchestration Engine may become a monolithic orchestrator if phase-gating logic grows to include subsystem-specific optimizations. Mitigation: enforce strict separation between phase sequencing (deterministic) and subsystem-level phase execution (adaptive); use code reviews and architecture audits to prevent logic creep.

Risk: Safety-critical phase-gating decisions may conflict with operational efficiency goals (e.g., minimizing cycle time). Mitigation: establish safety-first decision hierarchy; document trade-offs and operator override procedures; include safety validation in commissioning protocol.
evidence_needed: Formal specification of phase decomposition (FR4.1): phase definitions, preconditions, state transitions, gating logic, and out-of-order execution prevention.
Formal specification of phase-gating decision logic (FR4.2): decision rules, real-time constraint inputs, SafetyAlert trigger conditions, and cycle abort criteria.
Canonical metric schema for phase-relevant KPIs (FR4.3): phase_id, phase_duration_budget, phase_energy_budget, phase_state, budget_utilization_pct, and conformance validation rules.
Commissioning protocol (FR4.4): duty-cycle characterization procedure, phase budget computation, validation criteria, version control, and rollback mechanism.
Real-time constraint detection algorithm (FR4.5): budget violation detection logic, SafetyAlert trigger conditions, dynamic adjustment rules, and operator review procedures.
Telemetry schema and latency requirements (FR4.6): phase KPI definitions, collection frequency, aggregation logic, and latency bounds for real-time constraint detection.

Integration test plan: verification of phase-gating logic, subsystem interface conformance, budget violation detection, and cycle-time acceptance criterion (p95 ≤ CT_target) under site-specific duty cycles.
Commissioning validation report: measured phase budgets, subsystem capability validation, budget adjustment history, and acceptance sign-off for each site condition.
